transcript:
Nick Ducoff: Good morning everybody. Thank you so much for being here on day three, Saturday Breakpoint. Hopefully everybody has had an amazing time so far. I'm really, really grateful to be joined today by my friend Scott Lucas who has traveled from New York, then London, arrived just this morning. So let's wake him up. Let's do another round of applause for Scott.

So, it was hard to miss, but in case you did, JPMorgan took part in a historic 50 million dollar trade on the Solana public network. Galaxy was the issuer of commercial paper and Coinbase and Franklin Templeton took it down. I'm going to let Scott first start by talking a little bit about the mechanics of that. What was the trade?

Scott Lucas: Thanks Nick and appreciate the opportunity to be here. It's probably a bit surprising to have someone from JPMorgan on a stage using a public blockchain network, but I think it's probably one of many times we'll do this as we go forward. I certainly hope it is because I think there's opportunity here for the broader market. And in this particular case, this is what we call commercial paper. Commercial paper is an instrument that companies use to manage their funding over time. And you kind of think about debt instruments, there's bonds which are sort of longer dated, you've got medium term notes within the sort of two to three to four year period, and then you've got commercial paper which is typically inside a year. So if you've got things you need to fund inside a year, that's where we use this instrument. And they don't trade as much. They're easy to price. And they get syndicated out to the market. And we felt that was the right sort of instrument to start with when it comes to actually getting our toes in the water on public blockchain and finding the right issuers and investors. And so yeah, it was finding an issuer, finding a buyer, or buyers in this case, which was great to have both Coinbase and Franklin Templeton part of that. Great partnership across the group with Galaxy, Solana, and all the teams. And it was really a question of like, how do we start to prove out that capital markets, internet capital markets on Solana, is a thing that we think the market likes and we think the market can work to? So it was really a question for us as an arranger, we sit in the middle of these deals all the time, it's a big part of our business, we're a credit intermediary. In this case we were an arranger. We created the token, we allocated that token into the Galaxy wallet, and then that was distributed from Galaxy to Coinbase and to Franklin on the Coinbase platform and it was settled with USDC. So the whole mechanics of the actual instrument on chain, which was a nice milestone to cross.

Nick Ducoff: So just to reiterate... Amazing. So just to reiterate, JPM tokenized using the Token 22, Token Extensions program, on Solana, DVP settlement, on chain for stablecoins. I mean this is truly incredible. Would you say Scott, if you could have pointed this a year ago even, what would the probability have been in your mind to do something like this today?

Scott Lucas: A year from this time, a year ago from this time, I think it was a zero probability outcome. I mean, you know, there's a number of things that have changed in that year. Some of it has been around the sort of the rule set, some of it's been around just general market dynamics, some of it's been around sort of risk appetite. But, you know, we were in a situation for a long time in the United States where the opportunity was pretty compressed. And now the opportunity is wider and that's good because it allows us to understand what role this technology, and more importantly sort of a wider set of technologies, can have on the economy and where we think capital markets will go in the future. And we couldn't even ask ourselves that question a little while ago. So it's actually good to be able to figure that out and really do some work to think about what role can blockchain, public blockchain, stablecoins, other types of digital currencies play in the sort of global economy. Because it's really about, and we were talking about this before, like this is about growth. This isn't about, oh it's a cool technology, it's not about hey I want to do something different. This is about saying if I can make some of these markets more efficient, I guess. What efficient means? Efficient means it's lower cost. Efficient means that I can extend the opportunity to a wider set of participants. Then what that really means is I can mobilize capital across the market in a way that I can't necessarily achieve with the current market structure. That's an economic growth story because you get more companies with more access to capital being able to do more things. Like that's the point of doing this stuff. It's not about the tech necessarily or the difference to the current market. It's about adding new things that create a great outcome. And we really think that's a good opportunity for the United States and other markets to explore. And that's the difference between a year ago and now is we couldn't explore that.

Nick Ducoff: So this was a, you know, fairly complicated deal in the sense that there were a lot of parties involved. You had on the infrastructure side Solana, Circle, Coinbase. On the deal side Galaxy, Franklin, Coinbase. And then of course internally at JPM, the world's largest bank. How do you get something like this done?

Scott Lucas: Yeah it's really hard. Really hard. I think, um, but you know the good thing is as a franchise, like if you can make, and I think you know most institutions would be the same, if you want to do something new, make a sensible argument, highlight the value of that, bring people with you, demonstrate the sort of technical and sort of reasonable understanding of how you would execute, and then just work through the checklist to get things done. And in an institution like ours which is heavily regulated and large, like there's a lot of things on that checklist that we need to keep ourselves happy, we need to keep our regulators happy, we need to keep our clients happy. There's a lot of sort of components, you know, that are probably two or three degrees separated from what you might see as the original idea, maybe even more, that just take a lot of like validation that it's one, possible to be done, and two, it's kind of inside the risk appetite of what we think the right sort of footprint is for us and our clients. So that takes time. The good news is sort of once you've crossed that initial threshold, like you can build from there. And that's where we really see this being as a start point, not an end point. You know, like we've demonstrated this is possible. I think we sort of said during the week like we would expect to extend this opportunity further across a wider set of issuers, a wider set of investors, you know, a wider set of products. That's certainly our aim is to then continue to pressure test and extend the horizons of what we can do here and really figure out what role Solana, what role sort of, you know, the broader ecosystem can play in capital markets. And I think that's a really important question to go after.

Nick Ducoff: So we were talking about it this morning, you know there has been other corporate debt issued on chain. We think this might be the first commercial paper that's been issued, certainly on a public blockchain I think. How did you pinpoint this as the product?

Scott Lucas: So we've done, yeah, I mean, where do you start with capital markets? Good question. Like there's so many different things you could do. And we've done a fair bit of research over the last few years. Although we haven't been able to extend the horizons as much as we would have liked to until this year, we have been working on our own private chain. We looked at longer term, you know, we looked at a bond, we looked at a medium term note, we looked at what, you know, the demand side could do, what the rules were for them, where their risk appetite was. We looked at some of the secondary market considerations and it really did narrow it into like, let's start at the short end of the curve. The risk profile is manageable for the issuer and the investor. If something changes, the duration risk of having that change is limited. They don't normally trade as much as say some of the longer dated instruments, or certainly there's not enough time to trade them. And so that really felt like it was the right place to start. And I wouldn't be surprised if over time not just the product but the duration of that curve starts to grow. And you know that's kind of how markets evolve anyway.

Nick Ducoff: What's the reception been?

Scott Lucas: It's been really amazing. We've already had incoming from both sides of that equation, both investors and issuers, to learn more. There's been some expression of interest to do more. And that's been in like probably 16 business hours. So, you know, I think when you think about that as a start point, I expect we'll see more next week. And really that's the objective is to say to the market, look, you know, there are other ways to do this. We can add to the existing market infrastructure. We're not going to turn the whole thing on its head in a short period of time, and we shouldn't because if you go too fast and you do too much and you break something, you lose the opportunity. So being thoughtful, steady, but actually taking real risk is the way that we see that we can make a difference to how some of these markets work and how we can bring value to our clients and hopefully to the broader market structure. And that's only just, the noise is only just starting to sort of build there and I think we'll hear more and more over the coming days and weeks. And I think our plans will be able to be met because of that sort of priming the pump through this deal.

Nick Ducoff: Just to get a sense of scale, how deep is the CP market like in the traditional side, how much of this do you do a year?

Scott Lucas: Ah, I mean it's, there are, issuance is daily into the US market. And you know there are, I mean we did a deal on our private blockchain earlier this year which is great, has got some limitations around the extension etcetera, but you know that one company which they announced they launched a one billion dollar CP program. That's like, that's just for that tranche of that desk on this market on digital. So you think about that across the whole spectrum, you're a very, very deep triple figure billions market that's turning over for high frequency. So there's a lot to be done.

Nick Ducoff: So that's a great segue into why public blockchain and why Solana?

Scott Lucas: So the public blockchain thing I think is really important for us to understand. You know, there are some really positive things about public blockchain that we would like to understand more. I think it's some distance before full scale regulated financial markets can operate at scale on public blockchains. There's some questions to ask, to answer there. But we should absolutely be going that way. And there's a few reasons. One is, you know, as we understand the sort of resilience components and sort of how these things get connected, you know there's an argument to be made about sort of more accessibility. So that's interesting. More accessibility brings with it more risk so we answer those questions and doing things helps you answer those properly. You know, as a franchise we have a very, very big technology budget. If at some point in the future we get comfortable with the risk and we can ask ourselves the question, you know, do we want to use technology or run technology? Using it, if it's commoditized and cheap, might be a better option for us and our clients. So that again brings the sort of cost base down. And Solana was a pretty easy one to pick. I think, you know, we're very familiar with some of the other platforms out there. There's a real momentum behind the internet capital markets sort of story on Solana. And we felt that it was important for us to really understand how the technology works, how the platform works, how the ecosystem is growing, and what the opportunity looked forward. And I also think it's important for financial markets to have choice. And you know, demonstrating that Solana is in the game, it has some capabilities on a range of different avenues, I think was an important statement to make as well.

Nick Ducoff: So Scott, you very graciously joined us at Solana Accelerate in New York earlier this year. This is your first Breakpoint here in Abu Dhabi. Can you share your perception of Solana, not just the technology but the broader ecosystem and community?

Scott Lucas: I think it's really exciting. Like, you know, each time I come to one of these events there's a new avenue to explore. I was talking to a couple of people backstage around, you know, I met two people and both of them had some things I wanted to follow up on. So, you know, there's a lot of incredible innovation I think across this community. There's a lot of appetite for people to explore it. I think bring those two things together and you start to see like, that's where businesses come from. And I come back to the point around economic growth, like that's part of that broader story. So I think Solana is playing a real role in that and bringing that sort of ingenuity and innovation to sort of what the opportunity looks like. Not all of it's going to be fine for regulated capital markets. And that's okay. Like there'll be some stuff that's retail focused, some stuff that other markets focused. But there will be components that are really sort of important for us to understand and engage with and learn from. And you know, the, even if some of the stuff is a little bit out there for us to ever operate in, having some of those ideas and like dragging the conversation forward is really important because that's how markets evolve. And if components of some of those more ingenious, sort of adventurous ideas that are being put forward on Solana actually get sort of distilled into things that make sense for regulated markets, that's a great outcome. That's where innovation comes from, that whole debate. So it's great to be immersed in that and really listen and hear what's happening and we take some of those things away.

Nick Ducoff: Will we be seeing JPMorgan do more on Solana?

Scott Lucas: I certainly hope so. I certainly hope so. We've got plans to. You know, I think we've looked at the debt side of the equation. You know, we have some plans to extend again issuers, investors, product. I mean like let's see what the market demand is off the back of kind of doing this sort of initial deal. And I think there is demand there and we should really look to develop that further. I think it'd be great to explore what we can do on the equity side of the equation. There's a lot of activity around that in the United States so there's work to do to think about where we could take that and how we would get there, but I think that would be interesting to explore as well. And then, you know, once you've got the cash market moving, then you can start to explore derivatives in a more meaningful way. So, you know, there is a forward to explore and you know we certainly, well put it this way, getting work done on one platform is really, really hard. Getting it done on multiple platforms is super hard. I think for now we'll exploit what we've done with Solana and see where we go from there.

Nick Ducoff: So last question. Given that you assigned, would have assigned a zero probability of this happening a year ago and and and we got it done... Unbelievable we got it done. Thanks to your hard work and the many around you and the other organizations that participated. What are we going to see a year from now?

Scott Lucas: I think we'll start to see a little bit of depth and breadth across the debt side for sure. Whether we see longer duration or not, I'm not convinced we'll get there. I think we need to have a little bit more of a footprint in the market of tradable assets that and appetite to trade. And I think some of the infrastructure around the secondary market still needs to evolve. So I still think it'll be pretty much short end of the curve, OTC type stuff for a while. But you know when you think about you've got some stuff happening in private blockchain, you've got the crypto end, you've got this space in the middle which is like capital markets on public blockchain, they're both kind of leaking into the center. And so it's a bit hard to predict exactly what will happen, but I'm very convinced a lot will happen. And you know Solana is going to be a key component of that. So it'll be, you know, it's good to have the partnership and good to be part of the ecosystem and learning and contributing.

Nick Ducoff: Scott, enormous gratitude for what you and JPMorgan have done and we look forward to doing much more together. Thank you so much.

Scott Lucas: Likewise. Cheers. Thank you.

sources.title: J.P. Morgan Arranges Landmark U.S. Commercial Paper Issuance on Solana Public Blockchain for Galaxy Digital Holdings LP
sources.snippet:
J.P. Morgan Arranges Landmark U.S. Commercial Paper Issuance on Solana Public Blockchain for Galaxy Digital Holdings LP[Skip to content](#main)
Thursday 11 December 2025 5:02 pm
# J.P. Morgan Arranges Landmark U.S. Commercial Paper Issuance on Solana Public Blockchain for Galaxy Digital Holdings LP
![](https://www.cityam.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/Screenshot-2025-10-14-at-9.28.36-am.png?w=95&h=95&crop=1)
By:[Business Wire](https://www.cityam.com/profile/businesswire/)
Share
* FacebookShare on Facebook
* XShare on Twitter
* LinkedInShare on LinkedIn
* WhatsAppShare on WhatsApp
* EmailShare on Email
J.P. Morgan announced today that it has successfully arranged a U.S. Commercial Paper (‘USCP’) issuance for Galaxy Digital Holdings LP, an affiliate of Galaxy Inc. (‘Galaxy’) on Solana, purchased by Coinbase Global Inc. (‘Coinbase’) and Franklin Templeton. The transaction is one of the earliest debt issuances ever executed on a public blockchain, and among the first in the U.S. to harness blockchain for the issuance and servicing of securities, marking a significant milestone for financial markets globally.
J.P. Morgan acted as Arranger, created the on-chain USCP token and facilitated the delivery-versus-payment settlement of the primary issuance. Both the issuance and redemption proceeds will be paid in USDC stablecoins issued by Circle, representing another market first for the USCP market.
**Scott Lucas, Head of Markets Digital Assets, J.P. Morgan says:**“Today’s transaction is an important step toward understanding the role blockchain will play in the future of financial markets. This trade demonstrates institutional appetite for digital assets and our capability to securely bring new instruments on-chain using Solana. As a client-centric business, we remain focused on meeting the evolving demand for digital asset exposure while preserving the integrity of traditional markets.”
J.P. Morgan continues to redefine what’s possible in digital assets, driving the future of finance with secure, scalable, and transparent solutions for institutional clients.
This milestone marks Galaxy’s first commercial paper issuance. The on-chain USCP format strengthens the firm’s short-term funding capabilities and opens access to a growing base of institutional investors incorporating blockchain-based money-market instruments into their portfolios. Galaxy’s Investment Banking Affiliate, Galaxy Digital Partners LLC, acted as Structuring Agent on the transaction, and the securities were purchased by Coinbase and Franklin Templeton.
Jason Urban, Global Head of Trading at Galaxy, says: “This issuance is a clear example of how public blockchains can improve the way capital markets operate. By bringing our first commercial paper offering on-chain and helping structure one of the earliest U.S. transactions of its kind, we’re putting into practice the model we’ve long believed in: open, programmable infrastructure that supports institutional-grade financial products. We’re pleased to collaborate with J.P. Morgan, Coinbase, Solana, and Franklin Templeton to help bring these innovations into everyday market activity.”
Sandy Kaul, Head of Innovation at Franklin Templeton, adds: “We’ve entered a new era where institutions are no longer just experimenting with blockchain—we’re transacting on it in a big way. This investment supporting the innovative work of the Galaxy team, and every new institutional deal with onchain issuance, accelerates our collective journey toward a more open, efficient, and resilient financial ecosystem.”
Nick Ducoff, Head of Institutional Growth, Solana Foundation, commented:****“This issuance marks a major step in bringing the security and efficiency of public blockchains to institutional finance. Solana’s architecture makes it possible for firms like J.P. Morgan to arrange fina

[Solana](https://ambcrypto.com/category/solana-news/)# Wall Street goes on-chain: JPMorgan executes landmark debt issuance on Solana
2min Read
JPMorgan’s $50M commercial paper issuance for Galaxy on Solana marks a breakthrough for public-chain finance, with all settlement handled in USDC.
Posted:December 11, 2025
![Avatar](https://ambcrypto.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/IMG_2326-scaled-e1760632492733-80x80.jpg)
By:[Adewale Olarinde](https://ambcrypto.com/author/adewale-olarinde/)
Journalist
Edited By:[Jibin Mathew George](https://ambcrypto.com/author/jibin-george/)
![Wall Street goes on-chain: JPMorgan executes landmark debt issuance on Solana](https://ambcrypto.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/12/Solana-1200x675.png)
![Avatar](https://ambcrypto.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/10/IMG_2326-scaled-e1760632492733-80x80.jpg)
[Adewale Olarinde](https://ambcrypto.com/author/adewale-olarinde/)
Journalist
Edited By:[Jibin Mathew George](https://ambcrypto.com/author/jibin-george/)
Posted:December 11, 2025
Share this article
* [**](#)
* [**](#)
* [**](#)
* [**]()
* [**](https://ambcrypto.com/wall-street-goes-on-chain-jpmorgan-executes-landmark-debt-issuance-on-solana/#respond)
* [**Share](#)
* [**Tweet](#)
* [**](#)
* [
**
]()
* [**]()
* [**](https://ambcrypto.com/wall-street-goes-on-chain-jpmorgan-executes-landmark-debt-issuance-on-solana/#respond)
**
J.P. Morgan has completed one of the first-ever U.S. commercial paper issuances on a public blockchain —and it chose the Solana network to do it.
The bank arranged a U.S. Commercial Paper [USCP] issuance for Galaxy Digital Holdings LP, with Coinbase and Franklin Templeton purchasing the tokenized security.
The move marks one of the earliest debt issuances executed on a public blockchain and a major milestone in the institutional adoption of open networks.
The[official publication](https://www.jpmorgan.com/about-us/corporate-news/2025/jpmorgan-commercial-paper-issuance-solana-blockchain)didn’t mention the value of the issuance, but[Reuters](https://www.reuters.com/technology/jp-morgan-harnesses-blockchain-debt-issuance-amid-digital-asset-adoption-boost-2025-12-11/)reported that it’s worth $50 million.
> “This trade demonstrates institutional appetite for digital assets and our capability to securely bring new instruments on-chain using Solana,” said Scott Lucas, Head of Markets Digital Assets at J.P. Morgan.
## **Why Solana? Data shows a network ready for institutions**
[Solana](https://ambcrypto.com/predictions/solana-price-prediction)has spent most of 2025 building a compelling case for enterprise-level finance. According to[DeFiLlama data](https://defillama.com/chain/solana?transactions=true), Solana’s TVL and transaction activity have climbed steadily throughout the year, even as volatility hit broader crypto markets.
The chart shows:
* TVL rising from \~$6B at the start of 2025 toward \~$10–12B mid-year
* Transaction counts remaining consistently high
* A clear upward shift into Q4, coinciding with growing institutional experimentation
For a bank like JPMorgan, which requires throughput, cost efficiency, and deterministic settlement, Solana offers the lowest-latency public infrastructure currently available.
This issuance confirms that the chain is now being utilized for real-world financial instruments, not just crypto-native act

tags.impact_reason: JPMorgan's involvement in tokenizing a $50 million commercial paper on Solana's public network signifies a major institutional adoption and a significant step in Real World Asset (RWA) tokenization within the Solana ecosystem.

visual_idea.reasoning: I chose a high-tech, tokenized 'Commercial Paper' certificate as the Semantic Anchor because it's the specific financial instrument at the heart of the story. This object allows for a direct visual fusion of JPMorgan's institutional, physical world (represented by a solid, valuable material like obsidian) and Solana's digital, public network (represented by glowing, integrated circuitry). I included the text '$50M' as a holographic projection because it's the headline transaction amount, and presenting it diegetically highlights the digital, on-chain nature of this new form of settlement.
visual_idea.description: A cinematic, low-angle shot of a large, perfectly smooth slab of polished black obsidian—a futuristic representation of a commercial paper certificate—floating in a dark, minimalist digital vault. Intricate, glowing circuits in Solana's purple and green hues are etched into its surface, pulsing with light, symbolizing the Token Extensions. From the top edge of the slab, a crisp, stable holographic projection displays the text '$50M', casting a soft blue light onto the object. The background is a vast, dark space with faint, out-of-focus geometric grids and data streams, conveying the scale of the public mainnet.

final_prompt.prompt_text: A cinematic, low-angle shot of a monumental, perfectly smooth slab of polished black obsidian—a futuristic representation of a commercial paper certificate—floating majestically in a dark, minimalist digital vault. Intricate, luminous circuits in vibrant Solana purple and emerald green are deeply etched into the obsidian's surface, pulsing with an ethereal light, powerfully symbolizing the Token Extensions. From the top edge of the slab, a crisp, stable holographic projection of '$50M' emanates, casting a soft, cool blue volumetric light that gently illuminates parts of the polished surface and highlights atmospheric particles. The background recedes into a deep, vast darkness, punctuated by faint, out-of-focus geometric grids and subtle data streams that emphasize the immense scale of the public mainnet. The composition is vertical (3:4), emphasizing the colossal height and epic scale of the obsidian slab, with significant negative space at the top, making the artifact feel truly monumental and imposing. Rendered with hyperrealistic detail, featuring deep cinematic contrast, realistic materials, and an atmospheric haze that enhances the volumetric light rays. 8k, octane render, raytracing, physically based rendering (PBR), hyper-detailed, ultra-detailed, cinematic lighting.

No visible text or numbers. 8k, octane render, raytracing, hyper-detailed, photorealistic, cinematic lighting, ultra-resolution, realistic materials, sharp focus on subject, extreme bokeh.

editorial.headline: JPMorgan Executes $50M Commercial Paper Issuance on Solana Mainnet
editorial.standfirst: The bank’s use of Token Extensions to settle institutional debt in USDC marks a definitive pivot from private blockchain experimentation to public network adoption. This transaction validates Solana’s infrastructure for high-frequency, regulated capital markets.
key_takeaways: Galaxy Digital issued the $50M instrument, with Coinbase and Franklin Templeton purchasing the debt via JPMorgan’s arrangement.
The deal utilized Solana’s Token 22 standard to enforce compliance and facilitate Delivery-versus-Payment (DVP) settlement in USDC.
JPMorgan views this as the beachhead for 'Internet Capital Markets,' with plans to explore on-chain equities and derivatives.
editorial.article_body_markdown:
The narrative of institutional adoption has shifted from theoretical pilots to live execution. In a move that Head of Markets Digital Assets Scott Lucas admitted had a "zero probability" outcome just twelve months ago, **JPMorgan** has successfully arranged a $50 million commercial paper issuance on the **Solana** public network. By orchestrating a deal where **Galaxy Digital** acted as issuer and **Coinbase** and **Franklin Templeton** as buyers, the bank has effectively bridged the gap between traditional credit intermediation and public ledger infrastructure.

This transaction is notable not just for its participants, but for its mechanics. Utilizing **Solana’s Token Extensions (Token 22)**, JPMorgan was able to embed necessary compliance logic directly into the asset, ensuring a controlled distribution while leveraging the speed of a public chain. The choice of commercial paper—short-term debt typically maturing in under a year—was a calculated risk management strategy. It allows the bank to test the "pipes" of internet capital markets with an instrument that has limited duration risk, while still proving out Delivery-versus-Payment (DVP) settlement using **Circle’s USDC**.

For institutional investors, the "why" matters more than the "how." The strategic driver here is economic efficiency, not novelty. The "Internet Capital Markets" thesis posits that public blockchains can lower the cost of capital and widen access to liquidity in ways legacy infrastructure cannot. By moving high-volume, short-duration instruments like commercial paper on-chain, JPMorgan is betting that the commoditization of settlement technology will eventually offer a superior cost basis compared to maintaining proprietary tech stacks.

Looking ahead, the bank intends to widen the aperture. While the immediate focus remains on pressure-testing the short end of the debt curve, the roadmap includes potential expansion into equities and derivatives. The convergence is clear: private bank chains and crypto-native public networks are "leaking into the center," creating a hybrid market structure where regulated assets trade on permissionless infrastructure.
